entities.description: Furnivall's principal witness. The official record dates this English parchment Canterbury Tales manuscript to c. 1410 and locates the Tales at ff. 2r-255r, with Gamelyn at ff. 54v-65r. Its sequence is A-B alpha-F(Squire)-D-E-F(Franklin)-G-C-B beta-H-I. One lost leaf in quire xxx accounts for the Parson's Tale supply from Royal MS 18 C II.

holding.notes: Acquired by the British Museum with the Lansdowne collection in 1807.

entities.label: British Library Lansdowne MS 851

physicalDescription.dimensions: 340 x 210 mm; written space approximately 250 x 140 mm
physicalDescription.extent: 255 folios plus flyleaves and a stub
notes: Written in England in Gothic cursive/Bastard Secretary; the official description associates the language with the Gloucestershire-Worcestershire-Herefordshire border area.

reviewNotes: The primary lineage subject is the checked-in XML artifact; the metadata cataloging unit is Furnivall's manuscript-centered edition-level compilation.
Lansdowne MS 851 supplies the continuous base sequence, but every explicitly supplied reading is represented by its own passage-scoped relation.
Royal MS 19 C XI contributes Middle French comparison quotations only. It is not an English Canterbury Tales witness and does not make Middle French a work-level discovery language.

The exact BSB Part III carrier contains fourteen Ellesmere-derived woodcuts. Furnivall's appendix contents additionally advertises twenty-three Ellesmere tellers and six tellers plus six allegorical figures from Cambridge Gg.4.27; the latter advertised set was not found in the exact BSB part carriers.
Public access, private cache presence, copyright status, provider terms, and permission to publish are recorded independently for every layer.

lineage.summary: The General Corpus artifact is the pinned agz8236 XML, copied from University of Michigan item AGZ8236.0001.001. U-M made its uncorrected OCR and Level-1 TEI representation in 2006 from Frederick J. Furnivall's seven-part Chaucer Society edition of British Library Lansdowne MS 851. Furnivall transcribed the principal sequence from Lansdowne but supplied explicitly marked readings and appendix passages from twelve other English witnesses, retained Tyrwhitt's paragraph divisions in Melibee and the Parson's Tale, quoted Middle French comparison passages from Royal MS 19 C XI, and advertised visual material copied from the Ellesmere and Cambridge Gg.4.27 manuscripts. Seven complete BSB part bundles, later Thomson research files, Tyrwhitt scans, three complete public supporting-manuscript facsimiles, and two complete scans proving both advertised plate suites were issued in Furnivall's later 1885 Harley MS 7334 volume are cached separately; current public facsimiles were not verified for Lansdowne or the other supply witnesses.

catalogSummary.author: Geoffrey Chaucer; anonymous author of The Tale of Gamelyn
catalogSummary.date: Canterbury Tales composed c. 1387-1400; bound title-page range 1868-1879; seven parts issued 1868-1884
catalogSummary.title: The Lansdowne MS of Chaucer's Canterbury Tales

catalogingSubject.label: The Lansdowne MS of Chaucer's Canterbury Tales
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloging unit is Frederick J. Furnivall's seven-part Chaucer Society edition-level compilation represented by University of Michigan item AGZ8236.0001.001. Its principal sequence prints the Canterbury Tales and the collateral Tale of Gamelyn in the order of British Library Lansdowne MS 851, but it also incorporates explicitly marked readings and passages from twelve other English manuscripts, uses Thomas Tyrwhitt's paragraph divisions in Melibee and the Parson's Tale, quotes Middle French comparison passages from Royal MS 19 C XI, adds five source-specific textual appendices and a Christ Church Nun's Priest end-link, and advertises or includes visual material copied from the Ellesmere and Cambridge Gg.4.27 manuscripts. This record does not collapse that mixed editorial object into the abstract Canterbury Tales, Lansdowne MS 851 alone, any supplemental witness, the separately issued physical parts, the U-M OCR/TEI encoding, or the checked-in XML artifact.

extent.description: Twenty-four conventional Canterbury tales are represented in the principal sequence, and the 69 content parts expose its groups, tales, links, prologues, commentary, appendices, and visual subparts. Gamelyn is collateral and contributes zero to the item and headline verse totals. The XML itself contains 55 DIV2 units, 622 LG elements, and 18,361 L elements; twenty-five L elements occur inside notes, leaving 18,336 direct non-note L elements. Gamelyn contributes 2 LG and 906 direct L, leaving the headline 620 verse groups and 17,430 direct line elements in the Canterbury Tales and edition matter. The XML also contains 215 direct P elements and 756 PB milestones. These are encoding-representation metrics, not a new critical lineation and not a claim that prose serialized in P is verse.

formStatements.description: The edition combines verse tales and links, two prose tales, prose editorial matter, and non-textual visual apparatus.

scope.description: The twenty-two conventional verse tales, collateral Gamelyn, and verse textual appendices; prologues and links carry their own part-level form.
Serious and parodic constituent romances; Gamelyn remains unclassified pending dedicated scholarship.
Nineteenth-century editorial titles, notices, headings, and apparatus.
Rubrics, quotations, and embedded passages rather than the primary language of the edition.

notes: The sequence A-B alpha-F(Squire)-D-E-F(Franklin)-G-C-B beta-H-I is Furnivall's manuscript-centered order for this edition; it is not silently generalized as Chaucer's final authorial ordering.
Lansdowne remains the principal source even though marked passage supplies make several groups composite and one lost Parson leaf is supplied from Royal MS 18 C II.

Gamelyn is collateral edition content, not a twenty-fifth Chaucerian Canterbury tale. Its 906 direct L elements are included in the edition-level representation total but excluded from the twenty-four-tale count.

The brief Middle French Royal MS 19 C XI comparison material is supporting apparatus, not a substantive language facet of the cataloged compilation; its language and function remain scoped in lineage.

The complete Ellesmere and Gg.4.27 manuscript bundles are cached as supporting sources, while metadata and lineage locators point only to the exact discontiguous leaves relevant to the edition's actual or advertised visual apparatus.
Rights, public access, cache presence, exact download links, request routes, and provider terms are recorded per entity and access route in the linked lineage manifest.

summaries.value: Furnivall's Lansdowne edition presents the General Prologue and twenty-four conventional Canterbury tales in the manuscript's A-B alpha-F(Squire)-D-E-F(Franklin)-G-C-B beta-H-I order, with the non-Chaucerian Tale of Gamelyn embedded after the Cook's Tale. It is an editorially composite manuscript edition: marked readings and passages come from twelve additional English witnesses, a lost Lansdowne leaf in the Parson's Tale is supplied from Royal MS 18 C II, Melibee and the Parson's Tale retain Tyrwhitt's paragraph divisions, and separate textual and visual apparatus extends beyond the principal witness.

The principal sequence contains eleven manuscript-order groups and twenty-four conventional tales. Gamelyn is collateral and contributes zero to that count. A Christ Church Nun's Priest end-link appears on p. 700; appendix pp. 1*-8* print five alternative or supplemental links and passages. Fourteen Ellesmere-derived teller cuts are present in the checked Part III carrier, while a twenty-three-image Ellesmere suite and a twelve-figure Gg.4.27 suite are advertised in the appendix contents. Two complete scans confirm both full suites in Furnivall's later 1885 Harley MS 7334 carrier; only whether and where they accompanied the earlier Lansdowne issue remains unresolved.
